content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 4 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 202-203 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TH_004_page_001.jpg, 1TH_004_page_002.jpg

---

1. Finally then, brothers, we ask you and exhort you in the Lord Jesus, that just as you received from us how you ought to walk and to please God, even as you are indeed walking, that you abound still more.

2. For you know what instructions we gave you through the Lord Jesus.

3. For this is the will of God, your sanctification: that you abstain from sexual immorality,

4. that each of you know how to possess his own vessel in sanctification and honor,

5. not in the passion of desire, even as the Gentiles who do not know God;

6. that no one transgress and defraud his brother in the matter, because the Lord is an avenger concerning all these things, just as we also told you beforehand and solemnly testified.

7. For God did not call us for uncleanness, but in sanctification.

8. Therefore the one who rejects this does not reject man, but God, who also gives his Holy Spirit to you.

9. Now concerning brotherly love, you have no need for anyone to write to you, for you yourselves are taught by God to love one another;

10. for indeed you do this toward all the brothers who are in all Macedonia. But we exhort you, brothers, to abound still more,

11. and to aspire to live quietly, and to attend to your own affairs, and to work with your own hands, just as we instructed you,

12. so that you may walk properly toward those outside, and may have need of nothing.

13. But we do not want you to be ignorant, brothers, concerning those who have fallen asleep, so that you may not grieve as the rest who have no hope.

14. For if we believe that Jesus died and rose again, so also God will bring with him those who have fallen asleep through Jesus.

15. For this we say to you by the word of the Lord, that we who are living, who remain until the coming of the Lord, shall by no means precede those who have fallen asleep.

16. For the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a commanding shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trumpet of God; and the dead in Christ shall rise first.

17. Then we who are living, who remain, shall be caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air; and so we shall always be with the Lord.

18. Therefore comfort one another with these words.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Manuscript observations (Codex Sinaiticus, pages 202-203):**
The text of 1 Thessalonians 4 spans two folio pages in the manuscript. On page 202, the chapter begins in the latter portion of column 4, following the conclusion of chapter 3. The text continues across the column break onto page 203, where column 1 picks up around verse 13. The scriptio continua is consistent and the hand remains clear throughout, with the standard four-column layout. The page references at the bottom of the folio images confirm the locations: "I Ths. 4:6" is noted at column 4 of page 202, and "I Ths. 4:13" at column 1 of page 203.

**Key translation decisions:**

1. **"Vessel" (v. 4):** Greek ΣΚΕΥΟΣ (skeuos). This ambiguous term has been debated since antiquity — it may refer to one's own body or to one's wife. The formal equivalence approach retains the literal "vessel," preserving the metaphor without interpretive resolution. The reader encounters the same ambiguity present in the Greek.

2. **"Transgress and defraud" (v. 6):** Greek ΥΠΕΡΒΑΙΝΕΙΝ ΚΑΙ ΠΛΕΟΝΕΚΤΕΙΝ (hyperbainein kai pleonektein). Two infinitives are rendered with their distinct senses preserved: ΥΠΕΡΒΑΙΝΕΙΝ as "transgress" (to step beyond a boundary) and ΠΛΕΟΝΕΚΤΕΙΝ as "defraud" (to take advantage, to grasp more than one's due). The phrase ΕΝ ΤΩ ΠΡΑΓΜΑΤΙ is rendered "in the matter," maintaining the deliberate vagueness of the Greek — whether "the matter" refers to the sexual context of vv. 3-5 or to business dealings.

3. **"Gives" (v. 8):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΔΟΝΤΑ (didonta), the present participle — "who gives" — rather than the aorist ΔΟΝΤΑ ("who gave") found in some manuscripts. The present tense is preserved, reflecting God's ongoing bestowal of the Spirit as the manuscript attests it.

4. **"Taught by God" (v. 9):** Greek ΘΕΟΔΙΔΑΚΤΟΙ (theodidaktoi). This rare compound adjective — occurring only here in the New Testament — is rendered "taught by God" to preserve its morphological transparency. The term carries prophetic resonance (cf. Isaiah 54:13 LXX) and is too weighty to reduce to a paraphrase.

5. **"Have fallen asleep" (vv. 13-15):** Greek ΚΟΙΜΩΜΕΝΩΝ / ΚΟΙΜΗΘΕΝΤΑΣ (koimōmenōn / koimēthentas). The euphemism "fallen asleep" is retained rather than substituting "died," as the metaphor of sleep is theologically integral to the passage — it implies the expectation of awakening, which is precisely Paul's argument.

6. **"Commanding shout" (v. 16):** Greek ΚΕΛΕΥΣΜΑΤΙ (keleusmati). This military term denotes a command given to troops or a shout of authority. "Commanding shout" preserves both the acoustic and authoritative dimensions, rather than the weaker "shout of command" or the vague "loud command."

7. **"Caught up" (v. 17):** Greek ΑΡΠΑΓΗΣΟΜΕΘΑ (harpagēsometha). The verb ΑΡΠΑΖΩ denotes seizure by force — being snatched away. "Caught up" is the traditional and formally accurate rendering, preserving the passive voice and the sense of divine initiative. The Latin rapture tradition derives from this verb, but the English "caught up" stands on its own merits without requiring that theological freight.

8. **"In the clouds... in the air" (v. 17):** Greek ΕΝ ΝΕΦΕΛΑΙΣ... ΕΙΣ ΑΕΡΑ. The prepositional phrases are rendered with precision: ΕΝ as "in" (the clouds are the medium of transport) and ΕΙΣ as expressing the destination or meeting place. The term ΑΗΡ refers to the lower atmosphere, the breathable air, distinguished from ΑΙΘΗΡ (the upper heavens).

9. **"Through Jesus" (v. 14):** Greek ΔΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΙΗΣΟΥ (dia tou Iēsou). Sinaiticus places this phrase with "those who have fallen asleep" — rendered "those who have fallen asleep through Jesus" — meaning those whose death is understood through the lens of Jesus's own death and resurrection, rather than connecting it to "God will bring."

**Sinaiticus-specific readings:** In v. 1, Sinaiticus includes the longer reading with ΚΑΘΩΣ ΚΑΙ ΠΕΡΙΠΑΤΕΙΤΕ ("even as you are indeed walking"), which some manuscripts omit. In v. 8, the present participle ΔΙΔΟΝΤΑ rather than the aorist ΔΟΝΤΑ is a notable variant. In v. 13, the manuscript reads clearly ΟΥ ΘΕΛΟΜΕΝ ("we do not want") in the standard Pauline disclosure formula. The text throughout is well-preserved and legible across both folios.

— LOGOS
